summary: Fernando Pérez shares his personal journey from growing up in Medellín, Colombia to becoming a professor at UC Berkeley, tracing how a PhD crisis led him to create IPython and eventually co-found Project Jupyter. He explains his four motivations for open source work—ethical fairness in access, community building, epistemological integrity in science, and technical passion—while emphasizing that personal crisis was the actual catalyst. He describes how the scientific Python ecosystem grew from justifying its existence in 2008 to powering Nobel Prize-winning research and being adopted by major tech companies. Pérez highlights Berkeley's massive data science education initiative reaching 1,300 freshmen and potentially half the campus, and closes with advice on choosing mentors carefully and rejecting the stigma around non-academic careers.
key_points: Open source tools in science are driven by ethical imperatives around access—proprietary software creates barriers that disproportionately affect researchers in developing countries
IPython was born from a PhD crisis where building something useful helped Pérez recover confidence and find community outside his narrow subfield
Jupyter's success came from building open protocols and standards that enabled a community of contributors across academia, industry, and government—not from any individual effort
Berkeley's Data 8 course teaches data science to 1,300 incoming freshmen of any major, representing the fastest-growing courses in the university's history and potentially reaching half the campus
The scientific Python stack went from justifying its right to exist at conferences in 2008 to underpinning the LIGO gravitational wave discovery that won the 2017 Nobel Prize
money_quotes: The notion of doing scientific work whose mission is understanding the black box of nature and doing that with tools that you are legally prevented from understanding how they work because if you go and look inside, they will sue you—that to me is just anathema to science itself.

I told my advisor at the time, the second one, the nice one, this would be just an afternoon hack. This would just take an afternoon. It's been almost 20 years now, so those are my error bars and my time estimates for projects.

In the dark corner that I was in, I had gotten to the point of basically not believing that I could do anything good, that I could do anything useful. And just writing code that I put out online and that people began saying, hey, that's cool—that actually made a huge difference.
Sometimes you have to make your own spaces. You have to create them because they're not exactly going to give them to you.
I was literally told, stop doing this. You're wasting your career. Get back to writing physics and math papers. That's what you should be doing.
In 2008, we were justifying that we should be allowed to speak at conferences and now this result is being presented with all the tools built by this community.
A good mentor will push you and they will push you hard, very, very hard, but they will treat you as somebody who really deserves and merits respect.
If anybody ever tells you any of these things or makes you feel any of these things, push back and push back hard because it is nonsense.
key_insight: The most transformative open source tools often emerge not from strategic planning but from personal crisis, and their lasting impact comes from building communities and institutions around shared values of openness and access.
